content.text: ) is the official developer portal for building with the Claude API. It's the control panel for everything API-related—separate from Claude.ai (the chat interface). From the Console, you manage API keys, test prompts interactively in
, track token usage and costs, set billing/spending limits, run evaluations, manage team members with role-based access, and access model documentation.
for accessing the Claude API—the equivalent of OpenAI's Platform Dashboard or Google's AI Studio.

# Console First-Time Setup

## 1. Account Setup
- [ ] Create account at console.anthropic.com
- [ ] Verify phone number
- [ ] Add payment method
- [ ] Set monthly spending limit

## 2. Get API Credentials
- [ ] Navigate to API Keys section
- [ ] Create first API key
- [ ] Copy key immediately (not shown again)
- [ ] Store in environment variable or secrets manager

## 3. Test Your Setup
- [ ] Try Workbench for first prompt test
- [ ] Install SDK (Python or Node.js)
- [ ] Run hello-world API call
- [ ] Verify usage shows in dashboard

## 4. Security
- [ ] Add .env to .gitignore
- [ ] Set up separate keys per environment
- [ ] Configure alerts for unusual usage
When you log in, you'll see the main dashboard with the following key sections:
The left sidebar contains these main sections:
Generate, manage, and rotate API keys
Token usage, costs, and request analytics
Payment methods, invoices, and spending limits
API guides, model docs, and tutorials
Organize keys and usage by project/team
- Total token usage (input + output) - Current month cost - Request count - Active API keys
- Latest API calls - Recent Workbench tests - Key creation events
- Which models you're using most (Opus, Sonnet, Haiku) - Usage trends over time
- Monthly spend cap status - Warning if approaching limit
Generate new API keys with descriptive names
View key metadata (prefix, creation date, last used, status)
Create separate keys for each environment (dev/staging/prod)

See full request/response cycles before coding

field, set global instructions (e.g., "You are a helpful coding assistant")

# Workbench Prompt Testing Workflow

1. Define your use case:
   - "Summarize legal documents"

2. Test in Workbench:
   - System: "You are a legal assistant specializing in contract analysis"
   - User: Paste contract text
   - Model: Claude Sonnet 4.6
   - Temperature: 0.3

3. Evaluate response:
   - Is it accurate?
   - Does it follow the format you need?
   - Are key points captured?

4. Iterate:
   - Adjust system prompt
   - Add examples
   - Refine parameters

5. Export code:
   - Click "Export Code" → Python
   - Copy generated code to your project

- Model (Opus vs. Sonnet vs. Haiku) - API key - Time period (hourly, daily, monthly)

# Evaluation Workflow

1. Create evaluation suite:
   - Add 10-50 test cases
   - Define expected outputs or scoring criteria

2. Run tests:
   - Select models to compare (Opus, Sonnet, Haiku)
   - Run evaluations

3. Analyze results:
   - Which model scores highest?
   - Which is most cost-effective?
   - Are there edge cases where models fail?

4. Iterate:
   - Refine prompts
   - Adjust parameters
   - Re-run evaluations
